data.title: Singapore Airlines to run additional flights between Singapore and London
Sinotruk is Marching Full Speed Ahead
data.description:
China’s property market is currently nursing a hangover as China’s economy pulls in two different directions.

The government definitely was in "Bob The Builder" mode. In H1 25, infrastructure was the reliable workhorse, while the property market was still stuck in a major funk.

According to data from the National Bureau of Statistics of China, infrastructure investment grew by 4.6% y/y in the first half of 2025, pouring cash into massive highway and bridge projects that kept heavy-duty rigs busy.

On the flip side, real estate investment took a massive 11.2% dip in H1 25 as new home starts crashed. Long story short, even as fewer dump trucks were being sold to housing developers, Sinotruk’s heavy-duty trucks were still in high demand for building those new highways and bridges.

This split is exactly why Sinotruk pivoted their strategy in H2 25. They’re putting their chips on growing parts of the economy like logistics and "cold-chain" delivery.

Plus, with the International Monetary Fund (IMF) projecting global economic growth of 3.0% for 2025, Sinotruk is leaning hard into its "go global" plan to catch that international tailwind while the domestic property scene cools off.

Across the seas

According to their 2025 Interim Report, Sinotruk is going "all-in" on electric. They’re chasing that 200% growth in New Energy Trucks and pushing high-end models (the Sitrak) to squeeze out better profit margins (and win over international markets).

They’re grinding hard in Africa and Southeast Asia to keep exports alive since the domestic market is a bit of a rollercoaster. Tech-wise, they’re slapping AI and smart driving gear into everything to keep fleet buyers happy. Behind the scenes, they’re trimming the fat—cutting costs and scaling back their boring financial services to focus on building big, smart rigs.

Green gains

Sinotruk’s H1 25 scorecard looks solid: Revenue hit CNY 50.88 billion, which is a 4.2% jump from last year’s CNY 48.82bn. Their gross profit also climbed 7.0% to reach CNY 7.66bn, up from the CNY 7.16bn reported for the same period in 2024.

The company’s “bread and butter” segment, Heavy-Duty Truck (HDT), crushed it this period. It moved about 148,800 units (a 9% y/y jump), grabbing a 27.6% market share in China. Domestic sales alone spiked 19%, and that segment’s profit ticked up 3.6% to CNY 2.6bn.

New Energy Heavy Trucks was the real star. Sales exploded, represents a 220.3% increase compared to H1 24! They cleared 10,000 units by mid-year and now own 12.5% of that green niche. Light-Duty Trucks (LDT) also stepped up, bringing in CNY 7.25bn (up 7.1%) with 62,816 units sold—a 10.4% increase.

The sino-side up

Sinotruk’s stock has surged 68.7% surge over the last 12 months. It’s currently sitting at HKD 37.88 (CNY 33.3), putting its market cap at a cool CNY 88.5bn (USD 12.3bn).

Even with that massive rally, it doesn’t look overpriced; its 9.2x estimated 2027 P/E is actually cheaper than its 11.4x three-year historical average.

Investors on the lookout for dividend have plenty to smile about, too, with yields expected to climb from 4.3% in 2025 to a juicy 5.7% by 2027. While the price is technically hovering just above the CNY 31.34 average target (a +1.6% spread), the pros aren't worried. Twelve out of 15 analysts are still assigning a "Buy" rating.

The road ahead

First off, they’re obsessed with exports. Banking on Russia and Africa is great until a local currency craters or a war flips the script. If those markets catch a cold, Sinotruk gets the flu. Plus, geopolitics is a massive headache—one new tariff or trade war could tank their global mojo and squeeze profit margins.

Back home in China, the property slump means fewer construction projects, which translates to way fewer dump trucks sold. They’re also dumping cash into pricey EV and hydrogen R&D—a big bet that hasn't fully paid off yet. Finally, the competition is brutal. If rivals start a price war, Sinotruk’s margins are going to get squeezed hard.

Tata Steel Limited Opens Scrap-Based Electric Arc Furnace Facility In Ludhiana
data.description:
Tata Steel marked a historic milestone in the Company?s journey toward sustainable steelmaking with the introduction of its scrap-based Electric Arc Furnace (EAF) facility at Hi-Tech Valley, Ludhiana. Built with an investment of approximately INR 32 billion, the Ludhiana EAF has a capacity of 0.75 million tonnes per annum. Designed to achieve CO2 emissions less than 0.3 tonnes per tonne of steel, the plant represents a significant step in Tata Steel?s commitment to achieve Net Zero emissions by 2045.

Designed to support low-carbon steelmaking, the plant will use nearly 50% renewable energy. The facility will use 100% steel scrap as raw material, sourcing approximately 40% scrap from the Company?s steel recycling plant in Rohtak, Haryana. The plant would produce construction-grade steel rebar under the Company?s flagship retail brand ?Tata Tiscon?, which would enable Tata Steel to further augment its market presence in the construction segment.

data.title: Why is Unilever looking to sell its food business?
data.description:
LONDON, March 20 (Reuters) - Unilever is in talks with McCormick & Company about selling its foods business, in a potential deal that would bring together the British company's Hellmann's and Knorr brands with McCormick's Cholula hot sauce.

Such a move would mark an acceleration of efforts to reshape Unilever. More than one Unilever CEO has tried to refocus the company's portfolio by expanding in personal care and beauty, and selling some food brands.

The food business came under the spotlight again when the Financial Times reported that Unilever might spin it off, and had held merger talks with Kraft Heinz that had ended.

Unilever's shares, which were higher in early trade on Friday, had fallen to their lowest since July last year as investors and analysts worried that CEO Fernando Fernandez could be distracted from the day-to-day running of Unilever by the potential separation. And they questioned ?the benefits of such an action so soon after Unilever's protracted ice cream unit split.

HOW MUCH IS UNILEVER'S FOOD BUSINESS WORTH?

Unilever's packaged food business accounts for more than a quarter of group sales, but faces pressures from a shift away from ultra-processed products, competition from private label brands, and softer demand as the rise of weight-loss drugs changes consumer buying habits.

Home to Knorr bouillon powders and Hellmann's condiments, the division's underlying operating margin - which excludes the impact of foreign currency exchange rates - was 22.6% of revenue, outstripping the group's 20% margin last year.

The food business, which also makes Marmite spreads, reported an operating profit of 2.9 billion euros ($3.34 billion) last year, giving it an enterprise value of roughly 30 billion euros, according to Barclays estimates.

SLOWER TO GROW COMPARED WITH THE REST

The business, Unilever's second largest by sales after personal care, grew at 2.5% last year, more slowly than the rest of the group and well below the company's own mid-term goal.

Underlying sales growth at Unilever's foods division has lagged that of other units since the COVID-19 pandemic highs, repeatedly falling short of the company's annual goal of sales growth of between 4% and 6%.

Analysts and investors question the long-term prospects of the packaged food industry when politicians, including U.S. Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr, have highlighted the potential health risks of processed foods.

DEVELOPED MARKETS HAVE REACHED SATURATION

Part of the problem is that the business is operating in two contexts: developed and emerging markets. Unilever's food business is growing more slowly in North America and Europe than in countries such as India and parts of Latin America, where the group has a stronghold in food and private label products are less sophisticated, meaning they offer less competition.

"There is more growth in emerging markets, which accounts for 55% of food for Unilever, but it's still not enough to make up for Europe and the U.S. where the market is saturated," Barclays analyst Warren Ackerman said.

(Reporting by Richa Naidu; editing by Barbara Lewis and Jane Merriman)

By Richa Naidu

Dangote Demand Soars As War Disrupts Africa Fuel Supply
data.description:
African nations are scrambling to secure fuel from Nigeria's Dangote Refinery as war in the Middle East disrupts supply.

The refinery is now seeing a massive surge in demand from countries like South Africa and Kenya to prevent local energy shortages. Bloomberg's Jennifer Zabasajja reports on Horizons Middle East and Africa.
